elements.sent-2411-001: In 1975, Erd\H os and Sauer asked to estimate, for any constant $r$, the maximum number of edges an $n$-vertex graph can have without containing an $r$-regular subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-002: In a recent breakthrough, Janzer and Sudakov proved that any $n$-vertex graph with no $r$-regular subgraph has at most $C_r n \log \log n$ edges, matching an earlier lower bound by Pyber, R\"odl and Szemer\'edi and thereby resolving the Erd\H os--Sauer problem up to a constant depending on~$r$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove that every $n$-vertex graph without an $r$-regular subgraph has at most $Cr^2 n \log \log n$ edges.
elements.sent-2411-004: This bound is tight up to the value of $C$ for $n\geq n_0(r)$ and hence resolves the Erd\H os--Sauer problem up to an absolute constant.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, we obtain similarly tight results for the whole range of possible values of $r$ (i.e., not just when $r$ is a constant), apart from a small error term at a transition point near $r\approx \log n$, where, perhaps surprisingly, the answer changes.
elements.sent-2411-006: More specifically, we show that every $n$-vertex graph with average degree at least $\min(Cr\log(n/r),Cr^2 \log\log n)$ contains an $r$-regular subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-007: The bound $Cr\log(n/r)$ is tight for $r\geq \log n$, while the bound $Cr^2 \log \log n$ is tight for $r<(\log n)^{1-\Omega(1)}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: These results resolve a problem of R\"odl and Wysocka from 1997 for almost all values of $r$.
elements.sent-2411-009: To find regular subgraphs efficiently, we replace and improve substantial parts of the Janzer--Sudakov framework for the Erd\H{o s--Sauer problem.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Our strategies involve the algebraic techniques of Alon, Friedland and Kalai, the recent breakthroughs on the sunflower conjecture, techniques to find almost-regular subgraphs developed from Pyber's work, and, crucially, a novel random process that efficiently finds a very nearly regular subgraph in any almost-regular graph.
elements.sent-2411-011: A key step in our proof uses this novel random process to show that every $K$-almost-regular graph with average degree~$d$ contains an $r$-regular subgraph for some $r=\Omega_K(d)$, which is of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-012: Finding regular subgraphs with given degree is a fundamental problem in Graph Theory.

elements.sent-2411-393: However, for any $u\in U_L$, if we condition on the event that $u\in V(G')$, then each hyperedge containing $u$ survives with a greater probability, $(1-\eps)^{k-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-394: Furthermore, if our hypergraph is linear, then, for each $u\in V(G)$, conditional on $u\in V(G')$, the events that $e\in E(G')$ are independent over all $e\in E(G)$ containing $u$.
elements.sent-2411-395: Hence, the degrees of low-degree vertices will drop slower than the degrees of high-degree vertices, and we can expect the hypergraph to become more regular.
elements.sent-2411-396: Unfortunately, there is no known analogue of Corollary \ref{cor:AFK for hypergraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-397: Thus, we are unable to extend Theorem~\ref{thm:regular in almost regular to this setting, and instead make the following conjecture.}
